learningOutcomes: Describe the structure of an atom including protons, neutrons, and electrons
Explain atomic number, mass number, and isotopes
Understand electron shells and basic electron configuration
realLifeContexts: Why different elements have different properties
How radioactive isotopes are used in medicine
Why noble gases are unreactive
commonMisconceptions: Atoms are mostly empty space — not a solid sphere
Isotopes of the same element have the same chemical properties
Electrons orbit in fixed circular paths

learningOutcomes: Explain Bohr's model and its limitations
Write electron configurations using subshells (s, p, d, f)
Define quantum numbers and their significance
Understand the aufbau principle, Hund's rule, and Pauli exclusion principle
realLifeContexts: How atomic emission spectra work in neon signs and fireworks
Why laser technology relies on electron excitation
How spectroscopy identifies elements in distant stars
commonMisconceptions: Orbitals are fixed paths, not probability regions
Higher atomic number always means higher energy electrons fill first
d-block elements always have predictable configurations

learningOutcomes: Apply quantum mechanical model to describe electron behavior
Interpret atomic spectra using energy level transitions
Solve problems on ionization energy trends using atomic structure
Explain anomalous electron configurations of Cr and Cu
realLifeContexts: MRI machines using nuclear spin (NMR)
Photoelectron spectroscopy in materials science
Semiconductor design based on electron configuration
commonMisconceptions: Ionization energy always increases across a period without exceptions
All electron transitions emit visible light
Effective nuclear charge equals atomic number

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between ionic and covalent bonds
Explain why atoms form bonds using the octet rule
Draw simple Lewis dot structures
realLifeContexts: Why table salt (NaCl) dissolves in water
How water molecules stick together
Why metals conduct electricity
commonMisconceptions: Covalent bonds are always weaker than ionic bonds
All atoms obey the octet rule
Ionic compounds are always soluble in water

learningOutcomes: Apply VSEPR theory to predict molecular geometry
Differentiate sigma and pi bonds
Explain hybridization (sp, sp2, sp3)
Understand metallic bonding and its properties
realLifeContexts: Why CO2 is linear but H2O is bent — greenhouse vs. solvent properties
How graphite conducts electricity (pi bonds)
Why diamond is hard (sp3 network)
commonMisconceptions: All molecules with polar bonds are polar
Hybridization changes the energy of the atom
More bonds always means stronger molecule

learningOutcomes: Apply Molecular Orbital theory to explain bonding in diatomic molecules
Calculate bond order from MO diagrams
Explain resonance structures and formal charge
Relate bond properties to reactivity
realLifeContexts: Why O2 is paramagnetic (MO theory explains it, Lewis doesn't)
Benzene's exceptional stability from delocalization
Drug-receptor binding based on molecular orbital interactions
commonMisconceptions: Resonance structures exist at different times
Bond order must be a whole number
MO theory and VB theory always give the same predictions

learningOutcomes: Identify organic compounds and carbon's unique bonding ability
Name simple alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes
Recognize functional groups: hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, carbonyl
realLifeContexts: Why carbon forms the basis of life
Petroleum products in daily life (petrol, plastics)
How soaps and detergents clean (organic chemistry)
commonMisconceptions: All organic compounds contain only carbon and hydrogen
Isomers have the same properties
Organic chemistry only applies to living things

learningOutcomes: Apply IUPAC nomenclature to branched and functional group compounds
Predict properties from functional groups
Understand addition, substitution, and elimination reactions
Explain structural and stereoisomerism
realLifeContexts: Chiral drugs — why thalidomide caused birth defects (stereoisomers)
How polymers are made (addition polymerization)
Fermentation and ethanol production
commonMisconceptions: Structural isomers always have similar properties
Chiral molecules always rotate plane-polarized light in the same direction
Elimination always requires a strong base

learningOutcomes: Propose reaction mechanisms (SN1, SN2, E1, E2)
Predict products of named reactions (Grignard, Aldol, etc.)
Analyze reaction pathways using carbocation stability
Apply spectroscopic data (IR, NMR) to identify organic compounds
realLifeContexts: Pharmaceutical synthesis requiring specific stereochemistry
How retrosynthesis is used in drug design
Polymer chemistry for biodegradable plastics
commonMisconceptions: SN2 always favors primary substrates without exceptions
Carbocation rearrangements only occur in specific reactions
NMR chemical shift values are universal across solvents

learningOutcomes: Explain the concept of reversible reactions and dynamic equilibrium
State Le Chatelier's Principle
Identify factors that shift equilibrium position
realLifeContexts: How the Haber process makes fertilizers
Why increasing pressure on gases affects yield
Carbonate equilibrium in blood pH regulation
commonMisconceptions: At equilibrium, concentrations of reactants and products are equal
A catalyst shifts equilibrium position
Equilibrium means the reaction has stopped

learningOutcomes: Write expressions for Kc and Kp
Calculate equilibrium concentrations from Kc
Apply Le Chatelier's Principle to industrial processes
Distinguish between Kc and Kp and convert between them
realLifeContexts: Optimizing the Contact process for sulfuric acid
How blood buffers maintain pH using carbonate equilibrium
Industrial ammonia production conditions
commonMisconceptions: A large K always means a fast reaction
Temperature and catalyst both shift K
Q > K means forward reaction is favored

learningOutcomes: Solve multi-step equilibrium problems with small x approximation
Apply equilibrium to acid-base, solubility, and redox systems
Relate Gibbs free energy to equilibrium constant
Analyze coupled equilibria in biological and industrial systems
realLifeContexts: Why proteins denature at specific temperatures (thermodynamic equilibrium)
Solubility equilibria in kidney stone formation
Free energy and ATP hydrolysis in metabolism
commonMisconceptions: ΔG° = 0 means no reaction occurs
Ksp is always small for sparingly soluble salts
Buffer pH is always between 6 and 8

learningOutcomes: Explain oxidation and reduction in terms of electron transfer
Identify oxidizing and reducing agents
Describe the basic structure of an electrochemical cell
realLifeContexts: How batteries power devices
Rusting of iron as a redox process
Electroplating jewelry and coins
commonMisconceptions: Oxidation always involves oxygen
The anode is always positive
Reduction gains atoms, not electrons

learningOutcomes: Calculate cell potential using standard electrode potentials
Apply Faraday's laws to electrolysis calculations
Distinguish between galvanic and electrolytic cells
Balance redox equations using half-reaction method
realLifeContexts: How rechargeable lithium-ion batteries work
Electroplating in manufacturing
Chlor-alkali process for PVC production
commonMisconceptions: Electrolysis and electrolytic cells require AC current
Salt bridge allows electrons to flow
More positive E° always means faster reaction

learningOutcomes: Apply the Nernst equation to non-standard conditions
Relate cell potential to Gibbs free energy and equilibrium constant
Analyze corrosion mechanisms and prevention strategies
Evaluate fuel cell and battery technologies
realLifeContexts: Hydrogen fuel cells in electric vehicles
Sacrificial anode in ship hull protection
pH meters using concentration cells
commonMisconceptions: Nernst equation only applies at 25°C
Fuel cells are just better batteries
Corrosion only occurs in metals near seawater

learningOutcomes: Define reaction rate and explain factors affecting it
Describe the role of a catalyst
Explain collision theory
realLifeContexts: Why food spoils faster at room temperature
How enzymes speed up digestion
Why grinding coal dust can cause explosions
commonMisconceptions: Catalysts are consumed in the reaction
Higher temperature always increases rate without exception
All collisions between reactants lead to products

learningOutcomes: Write and use rate laws and rate expressions
Determine order of reaction from experimental data
Calculate rate constants and half-lives
Explain the Arrhenius equation
realLifeContexts: Radioactive decay as first-order kinetics
Drug metabolism and dosage calculations
Industrial reactor design using rate laws
commonMisconceptions: Reaction order equals stoichiometric coefficient
Half-life is the same for all reaction orders
A reaction with higher activation energy is always slower

learningOutcomes: Derive integrated rate laws and apply to complex data
Propose reaction mechanisms consistent with rate laws
Explain steady-state approximation and rate-determining step
Analyze enzyme kinetics using Michaelis-Menten equation
realLifeContexts: How enzyme inhibitors work as drugs (competitive vs. non-competitive)
Ozone depletion as a chain reaction mechanism
Catalytic converters and surface kinetics
commonMisconceptions: The rate-determining step is always the first step
Steady-state means concentration doesn't change at all
Pre-equilibrium requires the equilibrium step to be fast only

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between exothermic and endothermic reactions
Define enthalpy change (ΔH) and its sign convention
Explain specific heat capacity and heat calculations
realLifeContexts: Why hand warmers release heat (exothermic)
How instant ice packs work (endothermic)
Calorie content of food
commonMisconceptions: Exothermic reactions are always combustion
ΔH positive means product is stable
Temperature and heat are the same thing

learningOutcomes: Apply Hess's Law to calculate enthalpy changes
Use standard enthalpy of formation data
Calculate bond enthalpy from bond energy tables
Distinguish between ΔH and ΔU
realLifeContexts: Rocket fuel energy calculations
How Born-Haber cycle explains ionic compound stability
Calorimetry in food science
commonMisconceptions: Hess's Law only works for specific types of reactions
Bond enthalpy values are always exact
ΔH = ΔU always

learningOutcomes: Apply the laws of thermodynamics to chemical systems
Calculate ΔS, ΔG, and predict spontaneity
Use the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation
Explain entropy changes in chemical and physical processes
realLifeContexts: Why ice melts spontaneously above 0°C (entropy drives it)
ATP synthesis as a thermodynamically unfavorable coupled reaction
Refrigerators and Carnot efficiency
commonMisconceptions: All spontaneous reactions are fast
ΔG < 0 means the reaction will definitely occur
Entropy always increases in chemical reactions

learningOutcomes: Define coordination compounds and ligands
Identify central metal atom and ligands in coordination compounds
Write formulas and names of simple coordination compounds
realLifeContexts: Hemoglobin as a coordination compound with iron
Why some gemstones are colored (metal complexes)
Cisplatin as a cancer drug
commonMisconceptions: Coordination number equals valency of the metal
All ligands are negatively charged
Coordination compounds are the same as ionic compounds

learningOutcomes: Apply IUPAC nomenclature to coordination compounds
Explain isomerism in coordination compounds
Describe crystal field theory and d-orbital splitting
Relate color and magnetic properties to crystal field splitting
realLifeContexts: Why copper sulfate solution is blue (d-d transition)
Cisplatin vs. transplatin activity difference (isomerism)
MRI contrast agents using gadolinium complexes
commonMisconceptions: Geometric isomers always have cis and trans forms
Crystal field theory explains all properties of complexes
Stronger field ligands always cause greater splitting in all geometries

learningOutcomes: Apply valence bond theory and MO theory to coordination compounds
Predict geometry from electron configuration and CFSE
Analyze spectroscopic and magnetic data of coordination compounds
Evaluate stability constants and chelate effect
realLifeContexts: Design of chelation therapy drugs for heavy metal poisoning
Homogeneous catalysis using Wilkinson's catalyst
Photosensitizers in photodynamic cancer therapy
commonMisconceptions: Chelate effect is purely entropic with no enthalpic component
Inner orbital complexes are always low spin
Trans influence and trans effect are the same phenomenon
